name: Tanuki
description: Tanuki, also known as raccoon dogs, are mischievous and jovial shapeshifting creatures deeply embedded in Japanese folklore. They are often depicted as plump, jovial animals with large testicles, which are humorously exaggerated and imbued with magical properties. Tanuki are known for their playful pranks, often targeting unsuspecting humans with illusions and transformations. While generally harmless, they can be cunning and resourceful, using their shapeshifting abilities to trick people for food, sake, or amusement. Despite their trickster nature, Tanuki are also considered symbols of good luck and prosperity, often represented with a sake bottle and a promissory note, signifying financial success. They are believed to be able to bring wealth and good fortune to homes and businesses. Their connection to nature and their playful spirit makes them beloved figures in Japanese art, literature, and popular culture, representing both the whimsical and the auspicious aspects of the natural world. The image of the Tanuki is prevalent throughout Japan, often found as statues outside restaurants and shops, inviting customers with their cheerful demeanor and promise of good fortune. Their folklore is rich with stories of their antics, transformations, and interactions with humans, solidifying their place as iconic figures in Japanese mythology.
subtitle: Mischievous shapeshifting raccoon dogs
appearance: Tanuki resemble raccoon dogs, with bushy tails, short legs, and rounded bodies. They typically have brown or grey fur with distinctive dark markings around their eyes, resembling a mask. They are often depicted with a large belly, a sake bottle, and an outsized scrotum (often humorously exaggerated), which they are said to use for various magical purposes, such as transforming them into objects or using them as drums.
habitat: Tanuki are native to Japan and can be found in forests, woodlands, and rural areas. They are adaptable creatures and can also be found near human settlements.
behavior: Tanuki are generally nocturnal and solitary animals, although they may form small family groups. They are omnivorous and feed on a variety of foods, including fruits, insects, small animals, and human food scraps. In folklore, they are depicted as playful tricksters who enjoy playing pranks on humans, often using their shapeshifting abilities to deceive them. Despite their mischievous nature, they are generally considered to be benevolent creatures.

keyMyths.title: Bunbuku Chagama
keyMyths.description: A tanuki transforms into a tea kettle to help a poor man become wealthy. The kettle, still with tanuki features, performs tricks and attracts customers, bringing the man prosperity.

keyMyths.title: Tanuki's Testicles
keyMyths.description: Tanuki are often depicted with large testicles, which they use for various purposes, such as transforming them into boats, umbrellas, or other objects. This imagery is often used humorously in Japanese art and folklore.

sources.author: Michael Ashkenazi
